1.synthesis: Now faith is the assurance (hypostasis) of things hoped for, the conviction (elenchos) of things not seen (v.1). For by it the people of old received their commendation (v.2). By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible (v.3).
1.historical_context: MKT notes that the placement of the faith-definition immediately after the Habakkuk citation ("the righteous shall live by faith," 10:38) situates it within the prophetic tradition of the righteous remnant who trusts God against visible evidence. The twin terms hypostasis (translated "assurance," but also "substance," "foundation," or "underlying reality") and elenchos ("conviction," "proof," "evidence") suggest that faith is not subjective emotion but epistemic confidence — the settled certainty of the mind that what God has promised is real, with the same kind of hold on the unseen that the seen has on the physical eye. The phrase "not made out of things that are visible" in v.3 reflects the Jewish doctrine of creatio ex nihilo implicit in Genesis 1:1 and explicit in 2 Maccabees 7:28.
1.literary_structure: The three verses serve as the definition, authorization, and cosmic grounding of the faith that the entire chapter illustrates. Verse 1 defines faith; verse 2 establishes its credentials (the ancestors received commendation by it); verse 3 gives its first exercise (by faith we understand creation itself). The movement from definition → authorization → cosmic scope establishes faith as the appropriate faculty for understanding the entirety of the created order and the promised redemptive future — not limited to religious practice but the basic epistemic posture toward a God-made world.
1.theological_application: Gill on v.1: "The 'faith' here spoken of is not a mere moral virtue... nor a bare assent to anything revealed... but a special grace of God... consisting of a firm persuasion and expectation, upon the ground of divine testimony." Faith is not the subjective intensity of wanting something to be true but the rational response to reliable testimony: God has spoken, and faith is the appropriate response to that speaking. Chrysostom on v.3: "Faith needs a generous and vigorous soul, and one rising above all things of sense, and passing beyond the weakness of human reasonings... For it is not possible to become a believer, otherwise than by raising one's self above the common customs [of the world]." The first object of faith — creation ex nihilo — requires exactly this: no one observed the creation, no scientific method can verify it, yet faith receives it as given by divine testimony.
1.christology: The creation-faith of v.3 is christologically significant given the prologue's claim that the world was created "through" the Son (1:2). Faith that "the universe was created by the word of God" is, in Hebrews' christological framework, faith in the same Word who became flesh and entered the heavenly sanctuary as the community's high priest. The faith-catalogue that follows (vv.4–40) traces the history of those who trusted the divine promises before their fulfillment — they were all, in the letter's perspective, trusting the same God who would fulfill everything in Christ. Their faith was prospective; the readers' faith is retrospective from the cross but prospective toward the return; the object of both is the same Lord.

4.synthesis: By faith Abel offered to God a more acceptable sacrifice than Cain, through which he was commended as righteous, God commending him by accepting his gifts — and through his faith, though he died, he still speaks (v.4). By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death, and he was not found because God had taken him — before his taking he was commended as having pleased God; and without faith it is impossible to please God, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him (vv.5–6). By faith Noah, warned by God concerning events not yet seen, in reverent fear constructed an ark for the saving of his household, by which he condemned the world and became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith (v.7).
4.historical_context: Abel's story had developed in Second Temple tradition as the paradigmatic martyrdom — the first righteous sufferer killed for his faith. Gill on Abel: "by faith" Abel offered "not a greater number of sacrifices, nor a greater quantity of the same sort, but of a better quality; his were of the firstlings of the flock, and of the fat thereof." The quality of Abel's sacrifice reflected the quality of his faith — which was directed toward the promised Seed whose blood would speak better things than Abel's own (12:24). MKT notes that Enoch's translation generated the enormous Enoch literature (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, 3 Enoch) — Hebrews strips away the elaborate elaborations and grounds Enoch's commendation simply in faith: he pleased God, and pleasing God requires faith (v.6). Noah's construction of the ark in response to "events not yet seen" is the faith-definition of v.1 enacted: assurance of things hoped for, conviction of things not seen.
4.literary_structure: The three examples (Abel, Enoch, Noah) represent three modes of faith's operation: Abel's faith expressed in worship, Enoch's faith expressed in sustained holy living ("walked with God"), Noah's faith expressed in obedient action on an unprecedented warning. Together they span the pre-flood world, showing that faith was operative from the very beginning of human history. The principle inserted between Enoch and Noah (v.6: "without faith it is impossible to please God") is not parenthetical but the thesis of the entire catalogue: every example is an instance of this principle in action.
4.theological_application: Gill on v.6: "Whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists" — this is the minimum of faith, but it is not empty: in a world of idol-worship and polytheism, the confession that the God of Abraham exists is itself a faith-commitment with radical social and religious consequences. "And that he rewards those who seek him" — the second element is the corollary: belief in God's existence is not passive but active; the one who exists is one who notices and rewards seeking, which means seeking is not futile. Barnes on Noah: he "condemned the world" — not by verbal condemnation but by contrast; his obedience was the implicit judgment on those around him who did not believe. The ark-builder's very activity was a preaching of coming judgment to those who witnessed it over the decades of its construction.
4.christology: That Abel "through his faith, though he died, he still speaks" (v.4) anticipates the great blood-contrast of 12:24: Abel's blood cried out for vengeance; Christ's blood "speaks a better word than Abel's blood." Abel's faith pointed forward through his sacrifice — the animal sacrifice offered in faith anticipated the better sacrifice. His speaking-through-death is both the endurance of his witness and the forward-pointing of his sacrifice. Enoch's translation (taken up without dying) functions in the christological framework as a type of resurrection-and-exaltation: the life lived by faith, pleasing to God, is taken up into God's presence rather than being consumed by death. Noah's righteousness-by-faith anticipates the Pauline-Hebrews understanding that the righteousness that pleases God was always a faith-righteousness, never a works-righteousness.

8.pericope_label: The Faith of Abraham and the Patriarchs
8.synthesis: By faith Abraham obeyed the call to go to a place he was to receive as an inheritance, going out not knowing where he was going; he went to live in the land of promise as in a foreign country, dwelling in tents, for he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God (vv.8–10). By faith Sarah received power to conceive, even when she was past the age — and from one man came descendants as many as the stars and as innumerable as the grains of sand (vv.11–12). These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen and greeted them from afar — strangers and exiles on the earth, desiring a better country, a heavenly one; God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared for them a city (vv.13–16). By faith Abraham offered up Isaac when tested — he who had received the promises offered up his only son, reasoned that God was able even to raise him from the dead, and received him back as a type (vv.17–19). By faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things to come; Jacob blessed each of Joseph's sons; Joseph gave instructions about his bones at death (vv.20–22).
8.historical_context: The Aqedah (binding of Isaac, Genesis 22:1–19) was the most theologically dense narrative in all of Jewish tradition. Midrash Genesis Rabbah 56:4 discusses whether God's promise could be fulfilled after Isaac died; Rabbi Akiva argued that Abraham's reasoning ("God is able to raise from the dead") was already present in the narrative. The self-description of the patriarchs as "strangers and exiles on the earth" (v.13) participates in the Hellenistic-Jewish philosophical tradition: Philo developed the idea of the soul as a pilgrim in a foreign body seeking its true heavenly home. MKT notes that "God is not ashamed to be called their God" (v.16) reflects the profound inversion of honor-shame: the divine patron who publicly acknowledges lowly clients is extending social capital to them — God publicly claims those who publicly identified with his promises despite the appearances.
8.literary_structure: The section is structured by the expanding scope of Abrahamic faith: faith in the call (v.8) → faith in the place (vv.9–10) → faith in the promise of progeny (vv.11–12) → the general principle of pilgrim-faith (vv.13–16) → faith at the point of ultimate testing (vv.17–19) → faith extending through the three subsequent generations (vv.20–22). The pilgrim-faith reflection of vv.13–16 is the theological heart of the section: the patriarchs did not receive the promise because the promise was designed to be received in a city God was building, not a land they could inhabit. Their tent-dwelling was not a failure of inheritance but a deliberate posture of hope oriented toward what was being prepared.
8.theological_application: Gill on v.10: Abraham was "looking for a city which hath foundations — not Rome, called the Eternal City; nor Jerusalem literally; nor heaven, often called a city in Scripture; but the new Jerusalem... the church of the firstborn, the general assembly, the city of the living God." The "foundations" contrast with the tent: a tent can be struck and moved, but a city with foundations is permanent. Gill on v.19: "Abraham accounted that God was able to raise him up even from the dead; from whence also he received him in a figure" — Abraham's faith was resurrection-faith before the resurrection of Christ. Matthew Henry on the patriarchs' dying in faith: "They saw the promises afar off, and were persuaded of them, and embraced them — they had a firm persuasion and expectation of them... and gave them a hearty welcome."
8.christology: The "city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God" (v.10) reappears at the letter's eschatological climax as "the heavenly Jerusalem" (12:22) and anticipates Revelation's "new Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from God" (Revelation 21:2). The patriarchs' pilgrim-faith was faith in this city — faith, therefore, in the final state that Christ's priestly work opens access to. Abraham's reasoning that "God was able to raise the dead" (v.19) is proto-resurrection faith: the one who called Abraham also promised to make his seed innumerable, and death cannot stop what God has sworn. This faith is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, which is the firstfruits of the life the city represents and the proof that God's ability to raise the dead is fact, not mere theoretical capacity.

23.pericope_label: The Faith of Moses and Others
23.synthesis: By faith Moses' parents hid him three months when he was born, because they saw the child was beautiful and were not afraid of the king's edict (v.23). By faith Moses, when grown, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter, choosing rather to be mistreated with the people of God than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin, considering the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt (vv.24–26). By faith he left Egypt, not afraid of the king's anger, for he endured as seeing him who is invisible; by faith he kept the Passover and the sprinkling of blood, so that the Destroyer might not touch them (vv.27–28). By faith the people crossed the Red Sea as on dry land; by faith the walls of Jericho fell; by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she had given a friendly welcome to the spies (vv.29–31).
23.historical_context: MKT notes that Moses' refusal to be called "the son of Pharaoh's daughter" meant renouncing a social identity of extraordinary privilege — Egyptian royal adoption conferred rank, resources, and a future of leadership in the most powerful empire of the ancient world. The phrase "the reproach of Christ" applied to Moses (v.26) is a typological identification: Moses bore reproach as a representative of God's covenant people in a way that structurally anticipated Christ's bearing of reproach (Psalm 69:9; Romans 15:3). Rahab's inclusion is remarkable — a Gentile and prostitute who "welcomed the spies" by faith enters the faith-roll that includes Abraham, Moses, and David. The Midrash developed Rahab's story extensively and later traditions listed her among the most beautiful women of the OT.
23.literary_structure: The Moses section (vv.23–28) is the longest treatment of a single figure in the chapter, reflecting Moses's importance to the Jewish readers. Five faith-actions are listed: parents hiding him (v.23) → Moses refusing royal identity (vv.24–26) → leaving Egypt (v.27) → keeping the Passover (v.28). Each is oriented by its faith-object: Moses endured "as seeing him who is invisible" (v.27), making his life's choices in light of the unseen divine reality rather than the visible Egyptian power. The movement from Moses (vv.23–28) to the exodus generation (vv.29–30) to Rahab (v.31) traces faith from the mediator to the community to the outsider-incorporated-by-faith.
23.theological_application: Gill on v.26: Moses considered "the reproach of Christ" — that is, the reproach he bore for Christ's sake, or the reproach common to Christ and his people — "greater riches than the treasures of Egypt": "not only as it exceeded them in duration, being lasting and eternal, when the other are fading and perishing; but in quality, being spiritual and heavenly; and in the pleasure that attends it." The "fleeting pleasures of sin" are fleeting precisely because they are temporal and the pleasures of a world that is passing away. Moses chose on the basis of the eschatological calculus: what will endure > what will not. Gill on v.27: "He endured as seeing him who is invisible — as if he had a sight of God by faith, which is the substance of things not seen."
23.christology: "The reproach of Christ" (v.26) is the christological boldness of the chapter's most striking phrase: Moses bore the reproach of Christ centuries before Christ came. The typological reading is not merely metaphorical — Moses, as the mediator of the old covenant, endured the same category of rejection that Christ as mediator of the new covenant would endure. Both were rejected by the people they came to save, suffered shame for the sake of the divine mission, and were honored by God despite human rejection. The Passover-keeping of v.28 is similarly christological: Moses instituted the Passover "by faith" — faith that the blood on the doorposts would prevent the Destroyer's judgment, faith that pointed toward the blood of the one Paul calls "our Passover lamb" (1 Corinthians 5:7).

32.synthesis: Time would fail to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets — who through faith conquered kingdoms, enforced justice, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions, quenched the power of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, were made strong out of weakness, became mighty in war, put foreign armies to flight (vv.32–34). Women received back their dead by resurrection; but others were tortured, refusing release that they might obtain a better resurrection. Others suffered mocking and flogging, chains and imprisonment; they were stoned, sawn in two, killed with the sword; destitute, afflicted, mistreated — of whom the world was not worthy (vv.35–38). And all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised, since God had provided something better for us, so that apart from us they would not be made perfect (vv.39–40).
32.historical_context: The "sawn in two" of v.37 was universally understood in early Christian tradition as a reference to Isaiah's martyrdom under King Manasseh — the Martyrdom of Isaiah (a Jewish text preserved in the Ascension of Isaiah) describes this explicitly. MKT notes that "of whom the world was not worthy" reflects the apocalyptic inversion of worldly judgment: those who appear as wanderers and cave-dwellers rejected by the world are actually the ones by whose presence the world was honored. The "better resurrection" of v.35 refers to the Maccabean martyrs (2 Maccabees 6–7; 4 Maccabees 5–18) who refused release at the price of apostasy — they endured torture precisely because they expected a resurrection that made death bearable.
32.literary_structure: The section is structured in two halves: the victorious (vv.32–35a, those through whom faith achieved military and miraculous outcomes) and the martyred (vv.35b–38, those through whom faith was expressed in suffering unto death). Both halves are commended for faith — the criterion is not outcome but orientation. The victorious and the martyred alike endured "as seeing him who is invisible" (v.27). The conclusion (vv.39–40) reveals the structure's theological logic: all of these, both victorious and martyred, did not receive what was promised — because the promise required "something better for us," the christological fulfillment that the letter has argued for ten chapters.
32.theological_application: Gill on v.39: "And all these, having obtained a good report through faith — they were bore testimony to, by God himself, in his word; and are on record in the book of God; and by their works of faith have left a good name behind them — received not the promise": "The Messiah was not yet come, and the better covenant with its better promises was not as yet formally established." The faith of the OT believers was genuine and commended, but it was necessarily incomplete — they trusted in a promise that remained unfulfilled in their lifetimes. This is not their failure but their position in the history of redemption: they were living in the anticipation, not the fulfillment. Gill on v.40: "God had provided some better thing for us — than what was visible under the law; better promises, a better covenant, better sacrifices, a better way of access to God, a better country, and better inheritance."
32.christology: "Apart from us they would not be made perfect" (v.40) is the chapter's most christologically concentrated statement. The perfecting of the OT saints depends on the new covenant's fulfillment in Christ — they cannot be made complete without the completion that his death and resurrection accomplished. This means the entire OT faith-history was incomplete, pointing forward to a fulfillment it could not itself achieve. The "cloud of witnesses" that surrounds the readers (12:1) is not an audience of spectators but a community of those who lived in promise and now, through Christ's completed work, participate in what was promised. Their perfecting and the readers' perseverance are therefore linked: the community of faith spans both Testaments and is made complete in Christ, who is the object of all its faith.
